topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the conference room seemed to buzz directly inside my skull. Sarah, the department head, leaned forward, her expression a mixture of strained patience and utter disbelief. "So, you'll be taking over the client onboarding process next week. I need you to walk them through everything." My hands, gripping the edge of the mahogany table, were slick with a cold sweat. I could hear the distant hum of the air conditioning, but all I registered was the frantic drumming in my chest. Every word Sarah spoke felt like shards of glass scraping against my nerves. I tried to form a coherent sentence, but my throat constricted, producing only a dry, rasping sound. My vision tunneled, the edges of the room blurring into a nauseating swirl of beige and grey. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the nearest filing cabinet, but my feet were rooted to the polished floor.

[story 2]

"This is a big responsibility," Mr. Henderson said, his voice unnervingly calm as he outlined the training schedule. As he spoke, the room seemed to tilt. I kept blinking rapidly, trying to clear the sudden fog gathering behind my eyes. My palms were visibly damp, and I found myself unconsciously twisting a loose thread on my sleeve until the fabric dug painfully into my skin. Every instruction he delivered registered not as information, but as a barrage of overwhelming noise. My breath hitched, short and shallow, and I could feel a distinct tremor running through my legs, making it difficult to maintain any semblance of stillness. I looked at the projector screen, but the shapes swam—a meaningless cascade of bullet points mocking my inability to process the demands.

[story 3]

When the manager assigned the handover, the world narrowed down to a single point of intense pressure behind my sternum. I managed a jerky nod, my mouth feeling impossibly dry. "I... I can do it," I managed to squeak out, the sound thin and reedy. Internally, a wild, panicked energy was surging through me, making my limbs feel too long, too shaky. I started pacing the small space between the chairs, a restless, caged animal. My gaze darted from Sarah’s expectant face to the closed door, then back again, unable to settle on anything. The air felt thick, suffocating, and every muscle in my body was coiled tight, vibrating with an almost painful tension. I just needed the meeting to end.
